title_en: Pounding the Pavement Everywhere I Go
excerpt_en: Today I had a meeting in the afternoon. Someone I happened to meet in Lisbon in January, a Japanese person living in France, introduced me to someone who works in French service at the Ritz hotel in Paris, and we had a meeting.
body_en:
Today I had a meeting in the afternoon.

In January, in Lisbon

🇵🇹, I happened to meet a Japanese person living in France, who introduced me to

someone living in Paris who works in French service at the Ritz hotel, and we had a meeting.

My mission is

to expand FOMUS masu globally,

and if there's any possibility of getting people to use masu,

I'll go anywhere.

This time too, through that person and their connections,

there was apparently a major figure in the sake world,

and I was supposed to meet them,

but the scheduling was tight

and I couldn't meet them this time.

However, I was able to hear about potential opportunities for expanding masu in French gastronomy.

If things go really well,

FOMUS masu might be used

at sake competitions in Paris.

We're still at the very initial stage,

and nothing major has been decided yet,

but this coming May,

there's apparently a big sake event,

and I told them I'd like to be involved

in any way possible.

There's potential to make lots of connections in Paris,

and even if it's not work-related,

it's the perfect place for networking.

So I really want to find a way to participate.

These are the kinds of conversations we had this time.

Both the actions toward the art scene on my second day in Paris

and this sake-related outreach

were only possible because FOMUS

has been collaborating with both art and alcohol using masu, which is how these opportunities came about.

Everything I've done in Japan

has not been a waste at all, and those experiences and activities

are connecting to where I am now.

Also, since I used to work in service at a French restaurant,

the other person said they felt a sense of kinship with me, haha.

Also, people who work as hotel staff in France

apparently tend to go independent at some point,

and many end up working

as alcohol importers.

In other words, many of them deal in sake too.

Conversely, that means they become competitors,

so I'll continue focusing on masu as a "sake vessel,"

supporting those who serve sake,

avoiding competition and aiming to collaborate

with the strongest player.

For that reason,

meeting the major figure in the sake world

is important.

Unfortunately I couldn't meet them this time,

but Ireland and France are close,

so I plan to fly over again soon,

and I'd also love to participate in the event

being held in late May.

This is also something that only happened because I actually went there in person,

which is how I got to hear about these opportunities.

Once again, I feel the importance of pounding the pavement,

even in unfamiliar places.

After that,

I stopped by a Japanese traditional crafts shop

that someone had told me about before,

and they told me they were lacking "wooden vessels," and they found the masu story interesting.

These kinds of encounters are really important too.

The culture of people introducing and connecting other people

is well-established in Paris, and it's fascinating.

Compared to Dublin,

there are overwhelmingly more Japanese people here,

and many people doing business here too.

By entering the Japanese community (there are probably many of them),

there seems to be a path into the French community from there.

On the other hand, in Dublin, Ireland,

there aren't that many Japanese people,

and there are even fewer business owners, and few people connected to Irish business owners,

so things are currently quite difficult there.

The types of people who gather differ by country,

and this is really interesting.

These are things you wouldn't know without actually going there,

so I really feel that going in person to gather information and pound the pavement is incredibly important.

Tomorrow I'll head back to Ireland,

but this Paris trip has yielded a lot of results.

I'll write up the details in tomorrow's article!

excerpt_en: Well, it's my last day in Paris. That said, I don't really have anything to do outside, so I'm heading to the airport quite early. The direct flight from Paris to Dublin was incredibly expensive because of St. Patrick's Day, so I'm going via Paris to London to Dublin.
body_en:
Well, it's my last day in Paris.

That said, I don't really have anything to do outside,

so I'm heading to the airport quite early.

The direct flight from Paris to Dublin

was incredibly expensive because of St. Patrick's Day,

so it was ridiculously pricey.

Paris➡︎London➡︎Dublin

is the route I'm taking with a layover.

At Charles de Gaulle Airport,

there's only one lounge that accepts Priority Pass,

and it was far from my flight's gate,

requiring a shuttle bus to get there, but since I was there anyway, I decided to check it out.

After about 30 minutes I arrived,

and just as I was about to eat,

it turned out they only had snacks 😂

There wasn't a single proper dish.

Since I hadn't eaten anything,

I reluctantly ate some madeleines and potato chips.

The lack of real food was really disappointing 😢

That said, I can still work there,

so I got some things done.

🔻To London Luton Airport

From Paris to London.

Since the airlines were different,

for the connection I had to exit the airport first

and then go through immigration and baggage check again.

Going from "arrivals➡︎departures" is old hat by now, haha.

Luton also had a lounge,

so I used my Priority Pass to check it out.

This one actually had proper food,

and I was able to fill my stomach properly.

I'd heard that the Luton lounge gets crowded,

but when I went, it wasn't crowded at all,

so there was no waiting at the beer tap

or trouble finding a seat.

Then in the evening, London to Dublin.

As for accommodation,

I originally planned to get a hotel,

but with St. Patrick's Day approaching,

hotel prices were 3 to 5 times the normal rate,

so I asked a favor

and stayed at a friend's place.

This saved me about 40,000 yen

(roughly $270).

Of course, I bought souvenirs from Paris in return, haha.

Should I rent a place in Ireland again after this?

My Dublin stay is about 4 days.

After properly watching St. Patrick's Day,

I'll return to Japan temporarily and open

the art gallery I've been working on in Sagishima.

It's just the opening though,

it's still far from complete,

and I plan to evolve it little by little.

Then,

after finishing what I need to do in Japan,

I'll head back to Ireland once more.

I have a Working Holiday visa,

so I can stay for over 90 days and work there.

However, in my case,

working at a local shop isn't really the plan

for Ireland,

and while I can use the Working Holiday visa until July 28th,

whether I should actually stay in Ireland

until then is something I honestly don't know.

To expand the FOMUS brand,

collaborating with Irish whiskey

or Guinness beer would be amazing,

but beyond that,

it's hard to make a significant impact.

Furthermore, selling to local hotels

requires getting into the inner circle.

On top of that, regarding Ireland,

I've learned over these 8 months of living here

that interest in sake is still quite low.

Should I spend another 3 months (April to July)

in Ireland where masu is hard to expand,

or perhaps

get a Working Holiday visa for another country

and live there instead?

Living in a country with cheap rent

while working as a photographer shooting international tourists and Japanese tourists would be easier in popular countries other than Ireland.

*Dublin has too much cloud cover and rain, making it unsuitable for photography

*There aren't many obvious places to shoot photos

Candidates include Hungary, Czech Republic, and similar places.

If I lived in one of those,

I could keep rent relatively low.

And when needed for work,

I could still go to Ireland or France.

Or maybe Dubai.

Living in Dubai

and working as a professional photographer

shooting tourists.

If I could shoot about 5 to 10 sessions a month

at around 100,000 yen per session,

it would further improve my photography skills

and also connect to Masu Photo.

Also, I periodically get the urge to fly somewhere (I couldn't stay in Dublin for 3 months without language school),

so I'd be away from home often,

and paying rent during those times is painful.

But I do want to leave my suitcases somewhere.

In my case, the masu and camera take up a lot of space,

so a backpacker style

of traveling around everywhere isn't really feasible.

The reality is that my schedule from May onwards is still unclear.

Living in France for a bit could work,

living in Czech Republic or Hungary could work too.

Or even going to Thailand or Bali

without using a Working Holiday visa.

I still don't have enough money,

so I need to properly earn income,

and figuring all this out is really tough.

My style is to save as much as possible

while investing heavily when it's needed for business, but where to set up my base

for optimal business purposes is still unclear,

and I don't think it's something others should decide for me.

I need to make this decision myself,

but if somewhere in Asia or Europe

there's someone saying "Let's promote sake!"

or "Let's create a Japanese space!"

then as FOMUS,

I'm confident I can join in and help energize things.

To find those kinds of opportunities,

I feel I need to explore a bit more.

Beyond just thinking about business success,

I'm entering my 30s, so I also need to seriously think about

what I want for my own life.

Seeing as much of the world as possible while I'm young

and building a network of allies

is something I should do now,

and while I still have the energy,

I know I need to do what I can.

All I can do is "make the path I choose the right one,"

and I don't know how things will turn out,

but I'll take my time thinking this through.

title_en: How Can You Slip in Promotion in a Way That Feels Good?
excerpt_en: So, I got back to Dublin last night and the next morning it was time for the walking event! This walking event is one I organize myself. It's been going on for 7 months now, believe it or not. About 10 people gathered this time too.
body_en:
So, I got back to Dublin last night

and the next morning it was time for the walking event!

This walking event is

one that I organize myself.

It's been going on for 7 months now, believe it or not.

About 10 people gathered this time too!

As for recruitment and marketing,

I don't do any of it at all.

Anyone registered on the Meetup app

can join the event.

This is only visible to organizers,

but participant ratings for the event are very high,

and apparently it shows up

near the top of the algorithm.

There are several reasons for the high ratings,

but I've built a system where participants

can have one-on-one conversations,

making sure everyone gets to talk.

To do that, I go through the extra step of handing out

cards with numbers and letters written on them beforehand.

This has been incredibly well-received,

and from past experience, I've gathered data showing that many people carefully take their cards home with them.

Also,

I make a point of talking to every participant,

and many of them have been to Japan before

or are planning to go,

which is why so many of them join.

And every time, the questions I get are:

"Where should I go?"

"What places do you recommend?"

That's what they ask.

For about two and a half years, I was an address hopper,

traveling all across Japan

and meeting tons of people.

I have friends and fellow business owners all over Japan.

And then,

through the crowdfunding campaign I ran last year,

several companies became sponsors,

including sponsors for the Masu Photo photobook

and individuals who supported me personally.

With all that in mind,

I've been writing letters and numbers on the back of business cards from

Kaori-san's Hotel Senkei, who supported us through crowdfunding,

and handing them out to participants.

By doing this,

I can run the event smoothly

while naturally promoting Hotel Senkei.

Actually, even though I had quite a lot of business cards,

I've almost run out of them now, haha.

As for the advertising effect,

honestly,

I don't know if it'll produce results right away.

If people remember it when they visit Japan,

they might reach out to inquire again.

If I find an even better method,

I'll try that too,

but for now, this was born from thinking about

how to get people to happily accept business cards (promotion) at events I host,

and I'll continue

to update and improve it.

So, with all that going on,

the walking event wrapped up successfully!

🔻People I want to talk to, and people I keep my distance from

Since it was Sunday,

I went straight to the

Sunday Japanese meetup for the first time in a while.

It's at the same venue,

so getting there is super easy, haha.

Honestly, I enjoy talking to people

who are here on Working Holiday visas

or working locally,

so I look for those kinds of people.

Over the past six months, the crowd has changed quite a bit,

and it was mostly college students, haha.

It's not that college students are bad or anything,

but I don't feel the need to actively approach them for conversation.

The reason is

that I don't really have much I want to ask them (I'm not that interested).

And experience-wise,

I inevitably end up doing most of the talking.

In that case, interacting with local international people is far more meaningful

and also helps with English practice.

This time, there was a woman who had been living

in Ireland for 23 years,

and I spent the whole time talking one-on-one with her.

Obviously,

if a foreign woman

and a man who clearly isn't a student

are having an intense one-on-one conversation,

and if you don't know either of them,

as a college student

it would be hard to jump into that circle.

She told me about all sorts of interesting

spots in Ireland,

and I also got to explain FOMUS to her.

Talking and connecting with people

one-on-one like this is

incredibly important,

and at my current level, when there are two or more native English speakers, it becomes insanely hard for me to jump into the conversation, haha.

This is something where I need to improve my English quickly, so I'll continue putting myself in English-speaking environments.

🔻The eve of St. Patrick's Day

March 17th is St. Patrick's Day,

and today is the day before. Plus it's Sunday.

So naturally,

events were being held all around.

From the evening, I walked around the city by myself.

St. Patrick's Day draws people

from all over Europe

to Dublin,

and if I can find a way to approach

all those gathered people,

FOMUS will become even stronger.

So,

I need to carefully observe

what kinds of things people are wearing and carrying.

This is a once-a-year chance.

I'm not participating as a business this time,

but if I discover something useful here,

I might be able to do something for 2026.

Walking around the city,

there were all sorts of festivities happening.

I had some time,

so I watched a fire performance.

I was intrigued by the costumes and performance style

you don't see in Japan, and I ended up watching the whole thing.

*The performers were beautiful women too

Then I headed to Temple Bar.

The crowds were insane, haha.

And everyone was so hyped up!

This is Ireland, this is Europe, I thought,

with the festival energy making everything even more intense, haha.

After that, I went to Dublin Castle

and saw the green light-up.

Then, while waiting for my friends who I'm staying with to get back home, I had a Guinness at a nearby bar!

Guinness really is the best!!

excerpt_en: Well, today is St. Patrick's Day. St. Patrick's Day is an Irish holiday celebrated every year on March 17th, commemorating the death of St. Patrick, the patron saint of Ireland.
body_en:
Well,

today is St. Patrick's Day.

St. Patrick's Day is an Irish holiday celebrated every year on March 17th, commemorating the death of St. Patrick, the patron saint of Ireland.

Features of St. Patrick's Day

  1. A traditional Irish holiday
  • It's a national holiday in Ireland, with parades and festivals held across the country.
  • Dublin hosts a large-scale parade every year, attracting many tourists.
  1. An event celebrated worldwide
  • It's celebrated on a large scale in countries with significant Irish diaspora, including the United States, Canada, Australia, and Japan.
  • Major parades are held in New York, Chicago, Boston, and other cities.
  1. Green is the symbolic color
  • St. Patrick is credited with bringing Christianity to Ireland, and his symbol is the "shamrock (three-leaf clover)."
  • It's tradition to wear green clothing and accessories.
  • In Chicago, the river is dyed green for this day.
  1. Drinking Guinness beer and Irish whiskey
  • Celebrating by drinking Guinness at the pub is a classic tradition.
  • Traditional Irish dishes (Irish stew, soda bread, etc.) are also enjoyed.
  1. The legend of the Leprechaun and the Pot of Gold
  • The "Leprechaun," a small fairy from Irish folklore, makes appearances.
  • It's said that finding the "Pot of Gold" he guards will bring good fortune.

So, that's the gist of it.

Basically, it's Ireland's biggest festival,

happening once a year.

On this day, regular businesses are closed, apparently.

I knew there would be a parade,

so I walked toward Temple Bar, and traffic was already being diverted.

I knew the parade started at noon,

so I headed to a supermarket's eat-in area

with a great view of the parade route.

At 11 o'clock, one seat happened to be open

so I grabbed it.

This freed me from

the crowds

and the cold.

The downside is that the sound is muffled. *Since I'm inside a supermarket

Still, this was way better than

waiting outside alone.

By the way, the reason I was alone is that

the friends I planned to go with

all had work, like part-time jobs.

The service industry is at peak business,

so of course they're working! Haha.

So that's how I ended up

going to the parade alone.

But in a way, there was only one seat available,

and from a business perspective,

this position where I could observe the crowd was the best.

I could enjoy it without worrying about anyone else,

haha.

https://youtube.com/shorts/n2F2cBzybjg?si=4V64WkRFRR1VVARZ

🔻What I learned from watching the parade

Basically, everyone was wearing

something green.

The most common item was headbands.

They're really cheap-looking items,

but since you only use them for this event,

this level is probably fine, haha.

Then there's green clothing.

Many people were wearing St. Patrick's Festival

hoodies, so in a sense,

they're easy to take home as "souvenirs" too.

Also, something interesting was

that some people were wearing tiny plastic glasses hanging from their necks.

Honestly, I'm not sure what they put in them,

but seeing people with mini glasses around their necks, I'm certain there's room for FOMUS masu to break into this space.

The neck-strap masu is

already one of FOMUS's products,

and if we designed it for St. Patrick's Day

with a green cord,

it would become a stylish accessory.

This is an item I definitely want to sell at the 2026 event.

If we sold the neck-strap masu with a green cord

and transfer stickers as a set,

everyone could customize theirs however they like,

and I think they'd have a great time.

Also, even if some don't sell,

since the transfer stickers haven't been applied yet,

the neck-strap masu can be sold elsewhere too.

Up until now, once you burn a design into the masu,

if it doesn't sell,

it becomes dead stock.

But if we use stickers instead,

while the leftover stickers would be wasted,

the masu themselves can be repurposed.

Since everyone walks around wearing something green, they're essentially advertising for free. So during the St. Patrick's period,

if I could rent even just one section of a shop

in the city center,

I could sell there

and share some of the revenue with the shop,

or instead of money,

exchange it for drinks or something.

There are all sorts of ways to make it work.

The bottom line is:

(1) The culture of wearing green items is deeply rooted

(2) Small items that can be taken home as souvenirs

(3) Something that doesn't look cheap

If we can check all these boxes,

we can reach festival-goers from all around the world,

they'll take them back to their home countries,

and since everyone takes photos,

we get free advertising.

If we can secure this slot,

we just need to go all-out selling masu in Ireland

for just March 15th through 17th.

For 2026, I'm thinking of preparing just 100 units,

giving 20 to influencers in advance,

and selling the remaining 80.

Based on those sales,

we can customize for 2027 and beyond.

Well, this is all still a hypothesis,

so it needs to be tested.

Over the next year, I'll negotiate

to see if we can do this in collaboration with a major company,

and if that doesn't work, FOMUS will go it alone.

March is apparently when Guinness beer

sells the most in Ireland.

That's because tourists from around the world flock here

for St. Patrick's Day,

and one reason is that they visit tourist spots like the Guinness Storehouse and the Jameson Whiskey tour.

If we could collaborate with these two companies,

we'd be in a really strong position,

but right now, I don't know how to get into Ireland's business inner circle.

By the way, even Japanese-owned Japanese restaurants don't sell Guinness beer,

meaning they haven't been able to partner with them either.

So I need to

properly get into the Irish business circle

and clear that hurdle.

I don't know how to get in yet,

but there's a JAPAN event in April,

and since it seems difficult for me to exhibit this time,

I'll participate as a volunteer instead,

get my name and face out there, promote masu,

and work toward getting a booth in 2026.

If people find it interesting at the April event,

I could redirect any unsold masu from St. Patrick's Day

to sell at this JAPAN event too.

This area is definitely a business opportunity,

so I'm going to aim for it properly.

🔻What's becoming clear

This is still just a hypothesis,

but rather than selling on a permanent basis worldwide,

it might be better to create designs tailored to specific events

and sell them that way.

We'd sell masu as event merchandise and souvenirs, and those who become interested in masu itself

could order online.

With online orders,

original logo designs are possible too.

In other words, as a front-end strategy,

we sell at various festivals as souvenirs and gifts,

and the back-end connects to custom-made designs.

I'm not a masu manufacturer,

and I'm not an artist either,

which is exactly why I should think more freely.

Like the shift from engravings to stickers,

I need to do this without being trapped by outdated assumptions.

As someone who knows the usual Dublin scenery,

I was amazed by

St. Patrick's Day's

incredible drawing power,

and it really hit home that I need to properly leverage this for business, haha.

And if we're going to sell at major events around the world,

it's definitely better to partner with people

who are well-versed in those festivals.

This means

I need to properly embed myself in the local community, and this is something AI simply cannot do.

Little by little,

I'm starting to see how to compete globally.

If I'm going to sell masu, it needs to be as event merchandise, otherwise it won't resonate with people overseas who don't know what "masu" is.

Also, if we sell on a permanent basis,

selling masu by itself is too advanced,

so like the Masu-Game brand I'm building,

we need to lead with characters.

FOMUS, as a masu brand,

will engrave its brand name on some of those masu,

and with a wider range of designs than ever before,

if we can make them into merchandise and souvenirs that match each event, serving as a "communication tool" for festival-goers, that would be amazing.

This St. Patrick's Day

was a great opportunity that gave me major insights.

Going in person and seeing things with your own eyes really is important.

364 days left.

I'll use this year

to deliver results at the 2026 event 🔥

excerpt_en: St. Patrick's Day is over, and the streets of Dublin have completely returned to normal. The green hats, shirts, and face paint that were everywhere yesterday have vanished without a trace, and people's outfits are back to the everyday. The hustle and bustle of the city has calmed down too, with the usual Dublin scenery spread out before me.
body_en:
St. Patrick's Day is over, and the streets of Dublin have completely returned to normal.

The green hats, shirts, and face paint that were everywhere yesterday have vanished without a trace, and people's outfits are back to the everyday. The hustle and bustle of the city has calmed down too, with the usual Dublin scenery spread out before me.

However, walking through the streets, I noticed people rolling suitcases.

They were probably travelers who came from other countries to enjoy the festival.

With the festival over, they're heading back to their respective countries. This ease of movement is one of Europe's greatest charms. In a continent of connected lands and Ireland with its wealth of LCCs (low-cost carriers), crossing borders is just an extension of daily life.

So, with that,

Dublin's streets are back to their usual pace.

Hotel prices that had skyrocketed to festival rates have settled down too, so I booked a hotel for the night.

The reason was to be able to leave my luggage until late after checkout the next day.

I carefully chose a hotel with 24-hour front desk service that would hold my luggage at no extra charge.

These small details of planning

are surprisingly important for a life full of travel.

Then I retrieved my suitcase that I'd left at a friend's place in Ireland.

Since I'm temporarily returning to Japan,

I needed to sort through my belongings.

My suitcase was already packed to the brim.

The reason? It was stuffed with masu that I'd once imported. There was no way I could take all of them back to Japan. So I decided to leave the masu with my friend again.

In other words, for this trip home, I'm taking everything except the masu back to Japan.

I had a lot of things I hadn't been using for a while, including souvenirs from the Medoc Marathon last year.

I'll sort out the unnecessary luggage and head back to Japan.

Right now, I'm someone who holds a Working Holiday visa but doesn't have a place of my own in Ireland.

I ended my lease in January

and spent about 3 months traveling around European countries.

Through this I learned a lot,

but during that time, my suitcases were

being held by friends living in Ireland,

about once a month.

If I didn't have friends who could store my luggage, I would have had to pay tens of thousands of yen just for storage.

But

thanks to the goodwill of friends working in Ireland,

they held onto my suitcases while I was traveling around,

and for these 3 months, I was able to get around

without spending a single yen on luggage storage.

I'm truly grateful for this,

and they accepted my somewhat pushy way of doing things.

So, all in all,

it was a day of preparation, preparation, and more preparation.

excerpt_en: Well, it's the day before my temporary return to Japan. During the day, I worked on FOMUS-related tasks and had an online meeting for the "Sagishima Art Gallery" that's about to open. There's a mountain of things to do, but I'm tackling them in order of priority.
body_en:
Well, it's the day before my temporary return to Japan.

During the day, I worked on FOMUS-related tasks and

had an online meeting for

the "Sagishima Art Gallery"

that's about to open.

There's a mountain of things to do,

but I'm tackling them in order of priority.

Then in the afternoon,

I went out for drinks with a friend I met in Ireland.

Being able to drink from 2 PM on a Wednesday

is truly the best, and back in Japan

no one my age would be up for that.

But this is Ireland.

There are lots of people here on Working Holiday visas,

and even among working adults, some can make time easily.

Thanks to that,

I can go out drinking during super quiet hours.

Since 2021, I've been off

the conventional career track,

so drinking at this time of day with someone my age

is basically impossible in Japan.

Plus, opportunities to even drink with people my age

are almost nonexistent, haha.

Because the worlds we live in are just too different

to have a real conversation.

*Not that one is better than the other

I can't talk about marriage and things like that,

and they can't talk about overseas life or business.

That makes the conversation dull for both sides.

But people who come here on Working Holiday visas

and have worked at a company before

are generally between 22 and 30,

and they're people I never would have met in Japan.

The people I meet in Ireland

tend to have a high chance of being interesting to talk to,

and having gone out into the world,

they share a common language with me,

which makes conversations enjoyable.

And with a select few,

it won't just end with meeting here.

In the future, whether as friends

or as work partners,

I think we'll continue our relationship.

The person I went drinking with today

is someone who's going to run in this year's

Medoc Marathon with me in September.

The fact that she wants to run the Medoc Marathon means

she already has the "I want to try fun challenges!" mindset,

and I enjoy talking to her

and find it interesting to listen to her too.

She's the kind of 20-something I never met in the past 5 years.

Basically, when I was active in Japan,

I was mostly around people in their 30s and older,

and I rarely talked to people my own age.

The reason is simple:

there was no one my age who found my activities interesting.

*Except for masu models

That's why people who've been abroad at least once

and have various experiences

can talk about life overseas,

which makes for incredibly meaningful conversations,

and they tend to find my activities pretty interesting too.

🔻Also went to the Japanese meetup

We started drinking one-on-one at 2:30 PM

and didn't wrap up until 7:30 PM! Haha.

We both had things to do next, so we parted ways,

and I headed straight to

the Wednesday meetup as usual.

There too,

I could connect with lots of Japanese and international people.

There were regulars who've been here a long time

and newcomers too.

At these gatherings,

I don't bring up work topics much.

There are regular college students here,

and since I do things differently from typical work,

there's a high chance I'd just confuse people, haha.

I only share about 10% of what I do

and spend my time listening to others instead.

I ended up staying until about 11 PM,

then rushed back to the hotel to grab my luggage

and headed straight for the bus to the airport.

Once again, I'm sleeping at the airport tonight.

🔻Connection is everything

So, all day today

I was talking to people nonstop

through networking and meetings.

I won't be able to see the people living in Ireland

for about a month now,

so today I went all out

spending time on connection.

In Japan,

I was always hanging out with

interesting older guys,

and while that was naturally fun,

opportunities to connect with young people were, in a sense,

pretty much nonexistent in my life.

So being able to come out to another country and talk to people of all ages (especially younger ones) like this is really stimulating.

Also, in my case,

I'm on good terms with the staff

at the Japanese Embassy,

so I also have opportunities to connect with people who are older.

Life in Ireland

has allowed me to create

a situation where I can interact

with younger generations, my own generation,

and older generations,

and it's incredibly fulfilling.

The remaining question is how to

get into Ireland's business inner circle.

It seems like the entry point

isn't within the Japanese community,

and furthermore,

even Japanese business owners in Ireland are barely connected to Irish business people, which I've learned over these 8 months.

In other words, you can't get to

Irish business owners through

Japanese business owners.

So I have to find another way in.

For now, I have a few candidates in mind.

There's a Japanese festival event in April,

organized by an Irish person.

Unfortunately, FOMUS can't exhibit this time, but I'll participate as a volunteer

and make a strong impression with masu.

Then there's using LinkedIn as a social media tool to directly reach out to marketing and PR people.

At the moment, that's all I can think of.

But then again,

spending too much time in Ireland,

which doesn't have that big of a market,

isn't the right move either,

so I'll be aggressively approaching other countries too.

Right now, Paris and Dubai are the candidates.

The point is, I'm living somewhere in Europe,

and I can go anywhere anytime if called,

and even if I'm not called, I'll be ready

to jump on any interesting events.

Over the past few years,

the one thing I've honed is

the communication skills to naturally connect

with any generation and business crowd,

and I'm going to make the most of it!!

And from here, the next day or so

will basically be silent airplane life, haha.

It'll take 26 hours to get to Haneda Airport.

title_en: Back to Japan 🇯🇵 A Month of "Tradition Meets Play" and Co-Creating the Future with Perspectives Gained Abroad
excerpt_en: Over the course of one month from March to April 2025, I temporarily returned to Japan for the first time in about 10 months. After my overseas activities that began in Los Angeles, this trip home had two major purposes: the opening of the art gallery on Sagishima in Hiroshima, and the further expansion of the masu brand "FOMUS."
body_en:
Over the course of one month from March to April 2025, I temporarily returned to Japan for the first time in about 10 months.

After my overseas activities that began in Los Angeles, this trip home had two major purposes: the opening of the art gallery on Sagishima in Hiroshima,

and the further expansion of the masu brand "FOMUS."

With an event in Ireland coming up in late April, it was a whirlwind of a stay, but this month turned out to be an incredibly fruitful time for me.

A One-of-a-Kind Art Gallery Is Born on Sagishima

What I poured the most energy into during my stay in Japan was setting up the art gallery on Sagishima Island in Hiroshima Prefecture.

This gallery, created by renovating a shipping container, had all of us working right up until the opening. FOMUS friends from all over Japan, and even a university student I'd met in Ireland who happened to be back in Japan at the same time, came all the way from Dublin to Tokyo to Hiroshima, rolling up their sleeves to help out.

Surrounded by Sagishima's rich nature and warm people, we were able to bring the best creative work to life.

Why Sagishima?

Sagishima is a beautiful island in the Seto Inland Sea. Once home to about 3,000 people, the population has now dwindled to around 500, with an aging demographic. The reasons I chose Sagishima were the island's atmosphere and the warmth of its people, which I discovered through a friend's introduction, as well as a perfect location I had been personally seeking.

In this modern age where AI is evolving and replacing many things, what we value most in creating FOMUS projects is whether the "land, human connections, history, and memories" are all intertwined.

These are values that AI cannot replace, and Sagishima had all of them. The "inconvenience" of only being accessible by ferry means the very process of getting to the gallery becomes a "memory" -- an irreplaceable value in itself.

Additionally, it's an environment where activities would be difficult without engaging with local residents, making warm community ties essential. Furthermore, "NOT A HOTEL" is also planned for construction in part of Sagishima, meaning that affluent visitors and VIPs will increasingly visit the island -- another major reason for choosing Sagishima.

The Opening Is Just the Beginning -- Toward a Space of Co-Creation

The Sagishima Art Gallery has opened, but it's far from complete. From here, we'll all "co-create" the gallery together.

By 2026, we'll collect over 100 works of art and create ceiling art. As a business model, we'll offer gallery rentals and accept exhibition submissions. The main attraction, the "Masu Wall," offers a name-engraving masu service where you can purchase the right to have your name inscribed on the wall (limited to 300 units).

Revenue from these will be used to further upgrade the gallery.

We're currently seeking sponsors for the Sagishima Art Gallery. If you'd like to exhibit your work or support us, please don't hesitate to reach out.

Many people see "only accessible by ferry" as a disadvantage, but I consider it the greatest value. While many islands can be easily reached by car over bridges, by deliberately requiring effort, the process of getting to the gallery with family, friends, or a partner (the memories) also becomes part of the value the art gallery provides. The beautiful sea stretching out before you is also an element that creates an extraordinary experience.

In an age where everything has become convenient, value is born precisely by deliberately going through multiple steps. I'm looking forward to verifying this hypothesis with data going forward.

The Sagishima Art Gallery is basically unmanned, but events will be held periodically.

Please come visit.

A Journey Woven by Human Connections and the New Challenge of "Playing with Tradition"

After the art gallery opening on Sagishima,

I set out on a journey to various places.

Tokushima - Mima

After leaving Sagishima, I headed to Mima in Tokushima Prefecture. At the guesthouse "Nodokeya" in the historic Udatsu townscape, I enjoyed connecting with the owner, Shibata-san, and the interesting people who gather there. I've visited Mima many times, but every visit brings stimulating encounters, and I spent a wonderful 4 days.

To expand the masu brand "FOMUS" globally, I feel I need to see more of the world myself, and for the time being I don't plan to settle in one place. That's exactly why having a place where people say "Welcome back!" when I drop by is truly a blessing for a traveler like me.

And I'm convinced that as these kinds of communities connect with each other, even more interesting futures will emerge.

As I'm currently based in Ireland, I have a growing number of overseas friends who want to visit Japan, and I've been thinking about communities I could introduce them to when they come.

Shibata-san is really pioneering in that field, so I'd like to help energize things in whatever way I can.

Osaka - Nishinomiya - Kyoto

After that, I moved on to Osaka, Nishinomiya, and Kyoto. In Osaka, I went to the go-to restaurant of Sho-san, who always takes care of me, and enjoyed delicious food. In Nishinomiya, I reunited with old friends and enjoyed catching up over nostalgic conversations.

In Kyoto, I visited Petit Chalet, which has always been supportive, and even got to join in cherry blossom viewing, a seasonal highlight.

With works using "leaf" (gold leaf) from Japanese traditional crafts and more, it's a place where you can deeply feel Japanese culture. I also went to see historic ceiling paintings in Kyoto and went out for drinks with business owners who've been supporting me. It was a truly fulfilling time.

It's been about 4 years since I gave up having a fixed home and started living as a traveler. I've been all over Japan and around the world, accumulating countless encounters and lessons along the way.

In an era where SNS and AI have evolved to make "connections" easy to come by, I believe that real interactions -- meeting face to face, feeling the atmosphere, and communicating beyond words -- are truly irreplaceable.

That's why I'm convinced that there is rich value precisely in the "inconvenience" that takes time and effort, and that the traditional crafts and cultures that have been passed down over generations hold the keys to surviving in the times ahead.

Having encountered art from around the world, I've rediscovered the power hidden in Japan's "wa" (harmony) spaces, and as a symbol of that, I incorporated the "Masu Wall" into the gallery. And I aim to create a gallery that continues to be loved by the local people who support us.

The Joy of Running and a New Me

On Saturday April 5th and Sunday April 6th, I participated in the "Tsunagare JAPAN" event organized by GPS runner Shimizu-san. On the first day as a runner, and then partway through as a photographer.

In 2023, I created the Taiwan LOVE masu with Shimizu-san during the cycling-around-Taiwan project and accompanied him as a photographer for a month, but I never dreamed I'd actually be running myself.

However, the influence of the environment you place yourself in is tremendous. In 2024, I took on the Medoc Marathon (running 42km while drinking wine) and completed it.

In June, after Shimizu-san returned from Peru, we ran 37km in Los Angeles to create a GPS art of Lincoln, and then during this return to Japan I participated in both days of "Tsunagare JAPAN," running a combined total of about 23km and having a great time.

I'll be participating in the Medoc Marathon again in September this year, so I'm going to keep up my training.

The Charm of Japan Felt in Kyoto and the Future of FOMUS

I spent the final stretch of my Japan stay in Kyoto again.

During the remaining time before heading back to Ireland, I worked while making time to meet friends and business contacts in person.

I also toured Kyoto's tourist spots for the first time in a while and had the chance to talk with tourists from abroad. Learning about their travel schedules and how they experience Japan was incredibly valuable.

Living overseas has made me appreciate Japan's charm even more strongly. Compared to two years ago, my understanding of how to deliver FOMUS content to the world has changed dramatically.

I'll enjoy this evolution while further accelerating FOMUS's global expansion.

Age 30, A New Stage

I turned 30 on April 14th.

From hotel staff to photographer to FOMUS -- I've shifted my career, and since 2021, I've been co-creating "culture, expression, and connections" with people around the world, all starting from the traditional Japanese craft of masu.

So far, I've done Masu Photo shoots and photobooks in over 12 countries, developed accessories and card games, built AI-powered support services and photography production, and now established an art gallery on Sagishima in Hiroshima -- working across both culture and business, domestically and internationally. I can say without regret that I gave my 20s everything I had.

And now, FOMUS is moving to the next stage. I've been based overseas since 2024, and in 2025, I'll be deepening my roots locally to strengthen FOMUS's expansion even further.

In 2024, I was pioneering the outside world on my own, but I hadn't yet found a clear answer to the question, "Where are we heading together with the FOMUS team?"

However, through continued activity, what's become clear is

"Playing with Tradition."

Not "preserving tradition" but "playing with tradition."

Not "using it correctly" but "using it in fun ways."

As a result, a world where "you can express yourself" expands. The feelings of "this is fun" and "I want to express myself" transcend borders and language through the tool of masu -- I've witnessed those moments right before my eyes, time and again.

I want to deliver more of those moments to the world.

There are many people who "preserve" traditional Japanese culture.

That's exactly why, through FOMUS, I'll deliver it by "playing" with it.

Connecting with people around the world, warmly and with fun. That's the future I deeply wish to create through FOMUS.

Thank you for your continued support.

Back Overseas, Entering the Profit-Pursuit Phase

My work in Japan is mostly wrapped up, and I'll soon be returning to overseas activities. Living abroad is fun and stimulating, but if you ask me "Do you want to live overseas forever?" my current answer is "No."

Japan is just easier to live in, and the food quality is on a completely different level.

However, to keep creating interesting things, I need multiple income streams and a stable business foundation.

Seriously expanding the masu brand "FOMUS" requires a significant budget and workforce, and honestly, we're still short. That's exactly why I'm diving into the overseas world now.

Of course, traveling abroad doesn't guarantee immediate results.

There are plenty of misses too.

But thankfully, FOMUS's domestic team members are keeping things running well, which means I can spend more time facing "the outside world" as a leader rather than a creator.

In other words, my role is to protect, nurture, and expand FOMUS by doing the legwork of sales, building funds, and growing our team.

Based on the "research and seed-planting" experience overseas in 2024, from April 2025, I'm going to seriously pursue profit as well.

Wrapping Up a Productive Month

I temporarily returned to Japan in mid-March to open the art gallery on Sagishima Island in Hiroshima Prefecture.

Thanks to the support of many friends and allies, we successfully held the grand opening on March 30th.

After that, I traveled all over Japan -- Mima, Osaka, Nishinomiya, Kyoto, Tokyo, Kawagoe, Kyonan -- visiting people who have supported me since before I went abroad.

I went cherry blossom viewing, ran nearly 25km over two days, went out drinking with someone almost every night, made new connections -- it was an incredibly stimulating time.

There were many places I couldn't get to in just one month, but I'll save those for next time.

Through this trip, I really felt that my approach to overseas activities will change significantly.

Through my 2024 activities of actually visiting various countries solo, I realized there are stories and unexpected opportunities that you simply can't get from internet research alone. And I also keenly felt that whether you can seize those opportunities depends on whether you've been preparing day by day.

Overseas activities aren't always easy to get people to understand, but I'm going to deliver solid results.

Now it's time for another flight. Here's to a long journey!

excerpt_en: April 22nd. After about a month in Japan, I'm heading back to Ireland. This time I chose a ticket via Shanghai with about a 10-hour transit, so I went out into the city of Shanghai. I visited Yu Garden and the Bund. I used the maglev train from the airport to the city center.
body_en:
April 22nd

After about a month in Japan,

I'm heading back to Ireland 🇮🇪

This time I chose a ticket via Shanghai with about a 10-hour transit,

so I went out into the city of Shanghai.

I visited Yu Garden and the Bund.

I used the maglev train from the airport to the city center.

300 km/h is seriously fast!

After arriving,

I walked around the streets.

Since it was a short stay, I hadn't exchanged any cash,

and the street stalls didn't accept credit cards,

so I couldn't do any eating while walking around, haha.

Still, the scenery of Yu Garden was really wonderful.

Now THIS is a real Chinatown, I thought.

After that, I walked about 20 minutes to the Bund.

It was already getting a bit dark.

The nighttime Shanghai skyline is really beautiful!

I didn't see many Japanese people around.

After enjoying the city for a while,

with the last maglev train departing at 9:40 PM, I headed back to the airport a bit early.

I didn't want to waste money on cash exchange,

so I couldn't eat anywhere locally.

But since I have a Priority Pass,

I headed to the Shanghai airport lounge.

At the lounge,

they had a live kitchen service

where they prepared noodle dishes for you, and I was very satisfied.

Of course, the beer was free too!

Then, at 1 AM,

my flight to Manchester departed!

excerpt_en: April 23rd. Narita to Shanghai to Manchester to Dublin -- after multiple connections, I'm back in Ireland. In my case, I want to save on transportation costs, visit various airport lounges, and I'm not in a rush time-wise.
body_en:
April 23rd

Narita➡︎Shanghai➡︎Manchester➡︎Dublin --

after multiple connections,

I'm back in Ireland.

In my case, I want to save on transportation costs,

visit various airport lounges,

and I'm not in a rush time-wise,

so

taking a bit more time like this

to cut costs is the priority.

I can work during flights too,

so it's not much of a hassle.

I arrived in Dublin around noon

and headed to the hotel.

It's been about a month since I was last in Ireland,

and it's gotten so much warmer.

I took a light stroll around Dublin

(not that I haven't walked these streets a million times already)

to readjust my body to Dublin, haha.

Since it was Wednesday,

there was a Japanese meetup,

so I went for the first time in about 4 weeks.

I reconnected with the usual crowd

and also met and greeted some new faces.

It was a great time.

This Ireland stay won't be super long either,

but there's an event coming up where I need to bring my A-game, so I'll be prepping for that while also pushing forward with Japan-side work.

excerpt_en: April 24th. On this day, a friend of a friend from Japan asked me to do a "life story interview," so I agreed to be interviewed. I was in Ireland and the interviewer was in Japan, so I sat in front of my computer at 8 AM.
body_en:
April 24th

On this day, a friend of a friend from Japan asked me to do a "life story interview," so I agreed to be interviewed.

I was in Ireland and the interviewer was in Japan, so I sat in front of my computer at 8 AM.

During the interview,

I shared various things about

the story of my life,

and they also dug deep into my future vision.

This was essentially an interview for research material, and it doesn't seem like my life story will be published publicly. But opportunities to reflect on yourself are rare, and it's not often that someone digs this deep, so it was a great experience.

By the way,

the person who brought this opportunity to me

was someone from a company that's also a sponsor of my Masu Photo photobook, and it was a direct offer from them,

so I accepted.

I can't accept every request that comes my way,

but for opportunities like this, I make sure to say yes.

After that, I powered through administrative work!

There's never a day when the work runs out,

so when I have time for desk work, I go full throttle!

That was my day, haha.
